revision.comment: [[WP:AES|←]]新しいページ: '[[ファイル:Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|right|thumb|250px|[[箱根神社]]奥宮（元宮）]] {{神道}} '''奥宮'''（おくみや）と...'
revision.text:
[[ファイル:Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|right|thumb|250px|[[箱根神社]]奥宮（元宮）]] {{神道}} '''奥宮'''（おくみや）とは、日本の神道における神社の中の、ある社殿に対する他の社殿の関係を現す名称のひとつ。または、その関係にある社殿そのものをいう。奥社、上宮、あるいは元宮というときもある。
== 概要 == 同一の神社に複数の社殿があり、かつ社殿と社殿の間が非常に離れていて一見すると別のものにさえみえるような場合にこの語が用いられることが多く、社殿が複数ではなく単一である、あるいは複数であっても互いに至近距離にあるような場合にはあまり用いない。
使用の最も典型的な例として、山裾と山頂の二社に同一の祭神を祀る場合、山裾の神社（本社・本宮・下宮）に対して山頂の神社を奥宮・奥社・上宮という。また、元々、山頂に本殿が設けられたが、参拝に不便なため、麓に本殿を移し、旧本殿を奥宮とした神社もあり、このような場合、奥宮を「元宮」と呼ぶことがある<ref>[[箱根神社]]はこのケースに該当し、奥宮を元宮と呼ぶ。</ref>
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[神道]] * [[神社]]
{{DEFAULTSORT:おくみや}} [[Category:神道]] [[Category:神社]]

[[ファイル:Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|right|thumb|250px|[[箱根神社]]奥宮（元宮）]] {{神道}} '''奥宮'''（おくみや）とは、日本の神道における神社の中の、ある社殿に対する他の社殿の関係を現す名称のひとつ。または、その関係にある社殿そのものをいう。奥社、上宮、あるいは元宮というときもある。
== 概要 == 同一の神社に複数の社殿があり、かつ社殿と社殿の間が非常に離れていて一見すると別のものにさえみえるような場合にこの語が用いられることが多く、社殿が複数ではなく単一である、あるいは複数であっても互いに至近距離にあるような場合にはあまり用いない。
使用の最も典型的な例として、山裾と山頂の二社に同一の祭神を祀る場合、山裾の神社（本社・本宮・下宮）に対して山頂の神社を奥宮・奥社・上宮という。また、元々、山頂に本殿が設けられたが、参拝に不便なため、麓に本殿を移し、旧本殿を奥宮とした神社もあり、このような場合、奥宮を「元宮」と呼ぶことがある<ref>[[箱根神社]]はこのケースに該当し、奥宮を元宮と呼ぶ。</ref>

== 概要 == 同一の神社に複数の社殿があり、かつ社殿と社殿の間が非常に離れていて一見すると別のものにさえみえるような場合にこの語が用いられることが多く、社殿が複数ではなく単一である、あるいは複数であっても互いに至近距離にあるような場合にはあまり用いない。
使用の最も典型的な例として、山裾と[[山頂]]の二社に同一の[[祭神]]を祀る場合、山裾の神社（本社・本宮・下宮）に対して山頂の神社を奥宮・奥社・上宮という。また、元々、山頂に本殿が設けられたが、参拝に不便なため、麓に本殿を移し、旧本殿を奥宮とした神社もあり、このような場合、奥宮を「元宮」と呼ぶことがある<ref>[[箱根神社]]はこのケースに該当し、奥宮を元宮と呼ぶ。</ref>
うんこ！

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 05:08 (UTC)}} [[ファイル:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|[[富士山本宮浅間大社]]奥宮（[[富士山]]山頂）]] '''奥宮'''（おくみや）とは、日本の[[神道]]における[[神社]]の中の、ある[[神社建築|社殿]]に対する他の社殿の関係を現す名称のひとつ。または、その関係にある社殿そのものをいう。奥社、上宮、あるいは元宮というときもある。
== 概要 == 同一の神社に複数の社殿があり、かつ社殿と社殿の間が非常に離れていて一見すると別のものにさえみえるような場合にこの語が用いられることが多く、社殿が複数ではなく単一である、あるいは複数であっても互いに至近距離にあるような場合にはあまり用いない。
使用の最も典型的な例として、山裾と[[山頂]]の二社に同一の[[祭神]]を祀る場合、山裾の神社（本社・本宮・下宮）に対して山頂の神社を奥宮・奥社・上宮という。また、元々、山頂に本殿が設けられたが、参拝に不便なため、麓に本殿を移し、旧本殿を奥宮とした神社もあり、このような場合、奥宮を「元宮」と呼ぶことがある<ref>[[箱根神社]]はこのケースに該当し、奥宮を元宮と呼ぶ。</ref>
== 主な奥宮 == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|[[武蔵御嶽神社]]奥宮 Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|[[鹿島神宮]]奥宮 Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|[[白山比め神社]]奥宮 太平山神社.jpg|[[太平山神社]]奥宮 Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|[[大神山神社]]奥宮 Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|[[伏見稲荷大社]]奥宮 Mitumine005.jpg|[[三峯神社|三峯神社（秩父市）]]奥宮 Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|[[箱根神社]]奥宮（元宮） Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|[[大縣神社]]奥宮 Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|[[大宮浅間神社]]奥宮 Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|[[能郷白山神社]]奥宮 Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|[[恵那神社]]奥宮 Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|[[普天満宮]]奥宮 Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|[[宝徳山稲荷大社]]奥宮 Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|[[砥鹿神社]]奥宮 Kibune7286.jpg|[[貴船神社]]奥宮 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|[[枚岡神社]]奥宮（本宮） </gallery>

revision.comment: Created page with "{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such..."
revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} * {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}
{{Shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shrines]]

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>

It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. {{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. {{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>

== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. {{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} * {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} * {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}

revision.text:
{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} * {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>
== References == <references />
== See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} * {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>
== References == <references /> == See Also! == * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} * {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}

It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} * {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} * {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社}}
{{Shinto-stub}}

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|ja|富士山本宮浅間大社|12=draft}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|ja|富士山|12=draft}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shrine architecture|ja|神社建築|12=draft}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|deity|ja|祭神|12=draft}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社|12=draft}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社|12=draft}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Jingū|ja|鹿島神宮|12=draft}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|ja|白山比咩神社|12=draft}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社|12=draft}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|ja|大神山神社|12=draft}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|ja|伏見稲荷大社|12=draft}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社|12=draft}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|ja|箱根神社|12=draft}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社|12=draft}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社|12=draft}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社|12=draft}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社|12=draft}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮|12=draft}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社|12=draft}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|ja|砥鹿神社|12=draft}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|ja|貴船神社|12=draft}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|ja|枚岡神社|12=draft}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>
== References == <references /> == See Also == * {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道|12=draft}} * {{ill|Shrine|ja|神社|12=draft}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 05:08 (UTC)}} [[ファイル:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|[[富士山本宮浅間大社]]奥宮（[[富士山]]山頂）]] '''奥宮'''（おくみや）とは、日本の[[神道]]における[[神社]]の中の、ある[[神社建築|社殿]]に対する他の社殿の関係を現す名称のひとつ。または、その関係にある社殿そのものをいう。'''奥社'''あるいは'''奥の院'''と言うときもある<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[コトバンク]]}}</ref>。
== 概要 == 同一の神社に複数の社殿があり、かつ社殿と社殿の間が非常に離れていて一見すると別のものにさえみえるような場合にこの語が用いられることが多く、社殿が複数ではなく単一である、あるいは複数であっても互いに至近距離にあるような場合にはあまり用いない。
使用の最も典型的な例として、山裾と[[山頂]]の二社に同一の[[祭神]]を祀る場合、山裾の神社（本社・本宮・下宮）に対して山頂の神社を奥宮・奥社・上宮という<ref name=":0" />。また、元々、山頂に本殿が設けられたが、参拝に不便なため、麓に本殿を移し、旧本殿を奥宮とした神社もあり、このような場合、奥宮を「元宮」と呼ぶことがある<ref>[[箱根神社]]はこのケースに該当し、奥宮を元宮と呼ぶ。</ref>。
== 主な奥宮 == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|[[武蔵御嶽神社]]奥宮 Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|[[鹿島神宮]]奥宮 Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|[[白山比咩神社]]奥宮 太平山神社.jpg|[[太平山神社]]奥宮 Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|[[大神山神社]]奥宮 Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|[[伏見稲荷大社]]奥宮 Mitumine005.jpg|[[三峯神社|三峯神社（秩父市）]]奥宮 Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|[[箱根神社]]奥宮（元宮） Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|[[大縣神社]]奥宮 Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|[[大宮浅間神社]]奥宮 Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|[[能郷白山神社]]奥宮 Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|[[恵那神社]]奥宮 Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|[[普天満宮]]奥宮 Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|[[宝徳山稲荷大社]]奥宮 Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|[[砥鹿神社]]奥宮 Kibune7286.jpg|[[貴船神社]]奥宮 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|[[枚岡神社]]奥宮（本宮） </gallery>
== 脚注 == <references />
== 関連項目 == * [[神道]] * [[神社]] {{神道 横}} {{DEFAULTSORT:おくみや}} [[Category:神社]]

== 脚注 == <references />

== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|祭神|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|qq=}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The [[:en:Hakone Shrine|Hakone Shrine]] exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Okumiya]][[ja:奥宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11445659]] Wikidata name: okumiya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okumiya|qq=}} of the [[:en:Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] at the summit of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]]]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] for describing the relationship between certain [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shrine architecture]] buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|qq=}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The [[:en:Hakone Shrine|Hakone Shrine]] exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi-Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Mitake Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|[[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Jingū]] Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|[[:en:Shirayama Hime Shrine|Shirayama Hime Shrine]] Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiheizan Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|[[:en:Ōgamiyama Shrine|Ōgamiyama Shrine]] Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|[[:en:Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari-taisha]] Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsumine Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|[[:en:Hakone Shrine|Hakone Shrine]] Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōagata Shrine|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ena Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futemma-gū|lt=Futemma-gū|lt=Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futenma Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtoku Inari Taisha|qq=}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|[[:en:Toga Shrine|Toga Shrine]] Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|[[:en:Kifune Shrine|Kifune Shrine]] Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|[[:en:Hiraoka Shrine|Hiraoka Shrine]] Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>
