<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Okumiya]][[ja:奥宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11445659]] Wikidata name: okumiya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okumiya|qq=}} of the [[:en:Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] at the summit of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]]]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] for describing the relationship between certain [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shrine architecture]] buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|qq=}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The [[:en:Hakone Shrine|Hakone Shrine]] exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Okumiya]][[ja:奥宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Okumiya]][[ja:奥宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11445659]] Wikidata name: okumiya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okumiya|qq=}} of the [[:en:Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] at the summit of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]]]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] for describing the relationship between certain [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shrine architecture]] buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|qq=}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The [[:en:Hakone Shrine|Hakone Shrine]] exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Okumiya]][[ja:奥宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Okumiya]][[ja:奥宮]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Okumiya]][[ja:奥宮]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11445659]] Wikidata name: okumiya [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 08:46, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okumiya|qq=}} of the [[:en:Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha]] at the summit of [[:en:Mount Fuji|Mount Fuji]]]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in [[:en:Shinto|Shinto]] for describing the relationship between certain [[:en:Shinto architecture|Shrine architecture]] buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|qq=}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The [[:en:Hakone Shrine|Hakone Shrine]] exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi-Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Mitake Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|[[:en:Kashima Shrine|Kashima Jingū]] Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|[[:en:Shirayama Hime Shrine|Shirayama Hime Shrine]] Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiheizan Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|[[:en:Ōgamiyama Shrine|Ōgamiyama Shrine]] Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|[[:en:Fushimi Inari-taisha|Fushimi Inari-taisha]] Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsumine Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|[[:en:Hakone Shrine|Hakone Shrine]] Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōagata Shrine|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ena Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futemma-gū|lt=Futemma-gū|lt=Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futenma Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtoku Inari Taisha|qq=}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|[[:en:Toga Shrine|Toga Shrine]] Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|[[:en:Kifune Shrine|Kifune Shrine]] Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|[[:en:Hiraoka Shrine|Hiraoka Shrine]] Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okumiya|qq=}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|en|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|WD=Q653180|lt=Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shrine architecture}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|qq=}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|en|Hakone Shrine|WD=Q705310|lt=Hakone Shrine}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>

{{Refimprove|date=August 28, 2017}} [[File:Fujisan Hongu Sengen okunomiya.jpg|thumb|250px|right|{{ill|Okumiya|ja|奥宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okumiya|qq=}} of the {{ill|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|en|Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|WD=Q653180|lt=Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha|1=Fujisan Hongū Sengen Taisha}} at the summit of {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|WD=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}}]] '''Okumiya''' (奥宮) refers to a term used in {{ill|Shinto|en|Shinto|WD=Q812767|lt=Shinto|1=Shinto}} for describing the relationship between certain {{ill|Shinto architecture|en|Shinto architecture|WD=Q3080012|lt=Shrine architecture|1=Shinto architecture}} buildings at a shrine, or the building itself that holds such a relationship. It is also referred to as '''Okusha''' (奥社) or '''Oku-no-in''' (奥の院).<ref name=":0">{{Cite web|url=https://kotobank.jp/word/%E5%A5%A5%E5%AE%AE-451835|title=奥宮(おくのみや)とは|accessdate=2020-04-22|publisher=[[Kotobank]]}}</ref>
== Overview == The term is often used when a shrine has multiple buildings, and these buildings are significantly far apart, such that they may appear to be entirely separate shrines. Conversely, the term is rarely used if there is only one building or if multiple buildings are located in close proximity to one another.
A typical usage is when the same {{ill|Kami|lt=Kami|lt=Deity|ja|祭神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/deity|qq=}} is enshrined in two locations, such as at the base and the summit of a mountain. In such cases, the shrine at the mountain base (referred to as the main shrine, main sanctuary, or lower shrine) is contrasted with the shrine at the summit, which is referred to as the Okumiya, Okusha, or Kamimiya.<ref name=":0" /> Additionally, there are cases where a main sanctuary was originally located at the mountain summit but was later moved to the base for convenience of worship. The original sanctuary then became the Okumiya, which in such instances may also be called "Motomiya" (元宮).<ref>The {{ill|Hakone Shrine|en|Hakone Shrine|WD=Q705310|lt=Hakone Shrine|1=Hakone Shrine}} exemplifies this case, with its Okumiya referred to as the Motomiya.</ref>
== Prominent Okumiya == <gallery> 武蔵御岳神社 奥宮.jpg|{{ill|Musashi-Mitake Shrine|ja|武蔵御嶽神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Musashi Mitake Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Kashimajingu okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Kashima Shrine|en|Kashima Shrine|WD=Q706499|lt=Kashima Jingū|1=Kashima Jingū}} Okumiya Shirayama-okumiya in Hakusan.jpg|{{ill|Shirayama Hime Shrine|en|Shirayama Hime Shrine|WD=Q704702|lt=Shirayama Hime Shrine|1=Shirayama Hime Shrine}} Okumiya 太平山神社.jpg|{{ill|Taiheizan Shrine|ja|太平山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiheizan Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Ogamiyamajinja Okunomiya 08.jpg|{{ill|Ōgamiyama Shrine|en|Ōgamiyama Shrine|WD=Q251160|lt=Ōgamiyama Shrine|1=Ōgamiyama Shrine}} Okumiya Okunomiya of Fushimi Inari Grand Shrine.JPG|{{ill|Fushimi Inari-taisha|en|Fushimi Inari-taisha|WD=Q714828|lt=Fushimi Inari-taisha|1=Fushimi Inari-taisha}} Okumiya Mitumine005.jpg|{{ill|Mitsumine Shrine|ja|三峯神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mitsumine Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Hakone Mount Komagatake shrine Dsc05579.jpg|{{ill|Hakone Shrine|en|Hakone Shrine|WD=Q705310|lt=Hakone Shrine|1=Hakone Shrine}} Okumiya (Motomiya) Top of Mount Hongu 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōagata Shrine|ja|大縣神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōagata Shrine|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} Okumiya Mount Owarifujij top 2010-02-27.jpg|{{ill|Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|ja|大宮浅間神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōmiya Sengen Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Kumanohakusangongen 2010-4-4.JPG|{{ill|Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|ja|能郷白山神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nōgo Hakusan Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Shinto shrine Ena 2008-05-22.jpg|{{ill|Ena Shrine|ja|恵那神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ena Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Futenma-gū Cave.JPG|{{ill|Futemma-gū|lt=Futemma-gū|lt=Futenma Shrine|ja|普天満宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Futenma Shrine|qq=}} Okumiya Houtokuinari Nagaoka.jpg|{{ill|Hōtoku Inari Taisha|ja|宝徳山稲荷大社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hōtoku Inari Taisha|qq=}} Okumiya Toga Shrine - Okumiya.jpg|{{ill|Toga Shrine|en|Toga Shrine|WD=Q3530344|lt=Toga Shrine|1=Toga Shrine}} Okumiya Kibune7286.jpg|{{ill|Kifune Shrine|en|Kifune Shrine|WD=Q276779|lt=Kifune Shrine|1=Kifune Shrine}} Okumiya 枚岡神社神津嶽本宮.jpg|{{ill|Hiraoka Shrine|en|Hiraoka Shrine|WD=Q32422|lt=Hiraoka Shrine|1=Hiraoka Shrine}} Okumiya (Motomiya) </gallery>
